title: Availability, Disruption Budgets, Graceful Draining, Autoscaling, and Capacity · Guided Manifest Studio
summary: A platform pairing session predicts each API transition before producing the first working availability and autoscaling operating policy for availability, disruption budgets, graceful draining, autoscaling, and capacity. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of availability and autoscaling operating policy while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Translate service objectives into replica count, failure domains, topology placement, readiness, surge capacity, dependency limits, and recovery evidence.
Calculate minAvailable or maxUnavailable, unhealthy eviction policy, controller scale, simultaneous drains, and voluntary versus involuntary disruption limits.
Derive HPA desired replicas from requests, utilization, custom or external metrics, missing samples, tolerance, stabilization, and scale policies.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop K8s Replica Failure Domain Capacity
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop service has 6 replicas but all share one zone, a strict disruption budget blocks drain, and HPA oscillates because requests and metrics are wrong. Balance failure domains, PDB, rollout surge, autoscaling signals, stabilization, and capacity. Competency probe: Translate service objectives into replica count, failure domains, topology placement, readiness, surge capacity, dependency limits, and recovery evidence.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop K8s Replica Failure Domain Capacity.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for translate service objectives into replica count, failure domains, topology placement, readiness, surge capacity, dependency limits, and recovery evidence. For Commit to the mission model · Workshop K8s Replica Failure Domain Capacity, keep K8s Replica Failure Domain Capacity connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.text: A workshop service has 6 replicas but all share one zone, a strict disruption budget blocks drain, and HPA oscillates because requests and metrics are wrong. options.text: Three replicas always provide high availability regardless of nodes, zones, storage, or shared dependencies. That claim does not survive the commit to the mission model evidence boundary.

For commit to the mission model, copy one familiar manifest or kubectl command, omit context, API discovery, desired-versus-observed status, failure, rollback, and disposable-cluster transfer checks, then accept an Applied response as production proof.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop K8s Capacity Chaos Recovery Evidence
steps.instruction:
Append the Kubernetes evidence item headed "  # Evidence: k8s-capacity-chaos-recovery-evidence-jzi6b9". Tailor its availability-policy.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic API or manifest review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run kubectl, Helm, kubeadm, and cluster commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop K8s Capacity Chaos Recovery Evidence.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns K8s Capacity Chaos Recovery Evidence into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop K8s Capacity Chaos Recovery Evidence, keep K8s Capacity Chaos Recovery Evidence connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-capacity-chaos-recovery-evidence-jzi6b9
  - competency: k8s-capacity-chaos-recovery-evidence
    artifact: availability-policy.yaml
    decision: capacity chaos recovery evidence
    environment: shared multi-tenant cluster under Restricted Pod Security
    fault-injection: drop one metrics series during an autoscaling surge
    retained-proof: bound claim snapshot checksum and application consistency query
    case-key: jzi6b9
    change: |
      objective: 99.9-percent-availability
      replicas: 4-across-2-zones
      pdb: minAvailable-3
      hpa: autoscaling-v2-cpu-and-queue
      stabilization: bounded
      node-headroom: measured
      faults: drain-zone-loss-metrics-gap
    verify: kubectl get deployment,pdb,hpa -n intake -o json
    failure: Reject replica-count-only HA, PDB as involuntary-failure protection, limit-based HPA math, or autoscalers without capacity and feedback analysis.
    repair: Tie replicas and topology to objectives, calculate disruption and scaling, reserve headroom, inject lag and failure, and measure user-visible service.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.
